summary: Joel Spolsky briefly flags a growing problem with Stack Exchange's Area 51 site-proposal platform: an explosion of overly niche, overlapping, and redundant community proposals. He points readers to a longer discussion on the Stack Overflow blog about how the team plans to address this by merging similar proposals. The post serves as a redirect rather than a full essay, signaling that community fragmentation was becoming a real operational concern for the Stack Exchange network.
key_points: Area 51 was generating thousands of proposals for new Stack Exchange sites, many of which were too narrowly focused to sustain viable communities
Overlapping and redundant proposals were fragmenting potential user bases across similar topics
The Stack Exchange team recognized the need to actively consolidate and merge proposals rather than let organic growth continue unchecked
money_quotes: A pretty clear pattern has started to worry us: too many ridiculously niche proposals, overlapping proposals, and proposals that are already covered by an existing site.
key_insight: Unchecked community-driven site proposals lead to fragmentation, and platform operators must actively consolidate to maintain quality and critical mass.
